reasoning: There is exactly one remaining Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting before the July 1, 2025 resolution date, scheduled for June 17-18, 2025. The Fed has held rates steady at every 2025 meeting thus far (January 29, March 19, May 7). During the May 7 meeting, the central bank warned of "stagflation" risks, which initially reduced market expectations of a June cut from around 60% down to 34% (as measured by the CME FedWatch tool). Just days later (circa May 12-14), a U.S.-China "tariff truce" significantly improved the economic outlook, further lowering recession fears. Major brokerages (e.g., Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan) subsequently walked back their recession probabilities and downgraded their expected rate cuts, with some predicting only one late-2025 cut in December. As of May 12, futures markets priced the probability of a June rate cut at just 8.1%. Barring sudden, catastrophic economic deterioration before mid-June, the Fed is highly unlikely to cut rates before July 1. I am setting my prediction at 9% to closely mirror the forward market pricing while leaving an epsilon of uncertainty for rapid macroeconomic shifts.
